Category: Transportation & Logistics: Aviation, Maritime, Logistics, Shipping, Automotive
Description: Targeting navigation, scheduling, or control systems.
Prerequisites: A basic understanding of how transport systems operate (such as familiarity with aviation, maritime, or road networks) and a general awareness of cybersecurity concepts will help you get the most from this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify common AI-driven attack vectors that target navigation, scheduling, and control systems across aviation, maritime, and automotive sectors. You will also be able to explain how these threats are carried out and apply foundational protective measures to recognise and respond to suspicious activity within transport and logistics environments.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces learners to the growing threat of AI-driven cyberattacks targeting transport systems, including road networks, railways, aviation, and maritime operations. You will explore how malicious actors can exploit artificial intelligence to disrupt navigation systems, manipulate scheduling software, and interfere with critical control infrastructure. The lesson breaks down real-world scenarios in plain language, making complex concepts accessible even if you have no prior technical background. By the end, you will have a clearer understanding of how these attacks are carried out, why transport systems are particularly vulnerable, and what measures can help protect them.
lessons.content: Targeting navigation, scheduling, or control systems.

slides.subtitle: Targeting Transport Systems With AI Threats
slides.slidecontent:
• Transport systems increasingly rely on digital networks for navigation, scheduling, and control
• AI gives attackers powerful new tools to probe and exploit these systems at scale
• Attacks can target anything from traffic signals to railway management software
• Real consequences include delays, accidents, and public safety risks
• Understanding the threat is the first step toward defending against it

slides.subtitle: How AI attacks unfold systematically
slides.slidecontent:
• Attacker picks a target system
• AI scans for weaknesses
• Exploit is launched automatically
• System behaviour gets disrupted
• Attack adapts if blocked

slides.subtitle: AI threats to air traffic control and flight operations
slides.slidecontent:
• Air traffic control systems overwhelmed with false aircraft position data
• Flight management computers fed incorrect weather or routing information
• Airport ground control systems disrupted during critical takeoff and landing sequences
• GPS spoofing redirects aircraft away from intended flight paths
• Automated safety systems bypassed through coordinated signal manipulation

slides.subtitle: How AI exploits shipping and harbour operations
slides.slidecontent:
• Port management systems flooded with false cargo manifests and vessel schedules
• Ship navigation compromised through manipulated radar and GPS signals
• Automated loading systems disrupted, causing delays in cargo handling
• Maritime traffic control receives incorrect vessel position and speed data
• Supply chain coordination breaks down when shipping schedules are artificially altered

slides.subtitle: Targeting ground transport infrastructure
slides.slidecontent:
• Traffic signal timing manipulated to create congestion or accidents
• Railway signalling systems fed false train position data
• Public transit scheduling algorithms corrupted with fake passenger demand
• Smart motorway systems receive incorrect traffic flow information
• Vehicle-to-infrastructure communication compromised in connected transport networks

slides.subtitle: Common vulnerabilities across all transport modes
slides.slidecontent:
• Legacy systems with unpatched security vulnerabilities across aviation, maritime, and ground transport
• Real-time data feeds poisoned with false sensor information
• Remote access interfaces poorly secured, allowing worldwide exploitation
• Timing-dependent operations disrupted through coordinated delay tactics
• Integration points between different transport systems become attack vectors

slides.subtitle: Strategies to Safeguard Critical Transport Networks
slides.slidecontent:
• Patch systems regularly
• Monitor network traffic closely
• Train staff on cyber threats
• Use multi-factor authentication
• Isolate critical control systems
• Share threat intelligence widely

slides.subtitle: Core lessons for staying vigilant today
slides.slidecontent:
• AI targets transport systems daily
• Navigation and schedules can be manipulated
• Small disruptions cause big consequences
• Stay alert to unusual system behaviour
• Report anomalies to your security team
• Learn, share, and stay prepared

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI-driven attacks on transport systems. Over the next few lessons, we are going to explore a threat that is growing rapidly and one that affects all of us in our daily lives. Modern transport relies heavily on digital technology. Whether it is the signals controlling your morning commute, the software managing a railway network, or the systems coordinating air traffic, almost everything is connected. And where there is connectivity, there is vulnerability. What makes this particularly concerning today is that artificial intelligence is giving attackers far more sophisticated ways to find and exploit weaknesses in these systems, and they can do it faster and at a much greater scale than ever before. The consequences are not just technical headaches. We are talking about real-world disruption, potential accidents, and genuine risks to public safety. So throughout this course, our goal is to help you understand exactly how these threats work, because that understanding is the foundation of any effective defence.

So let's look at how one of these AI-driven attacks actually unfolds in practice. It starts with the attacker choosing a specific target, perhaps a rail network, a traffic management system, or a port. Once that target is selected, AI tools go to work scanning the system, probing for any vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, or weak points, and doing so far faster than any human could manage manually. When a weakness is found, the attack is launched automatically, no waiting around, no manual intervention needed. From there, the goal is disruption, causing the system to behave in unexpected or dangerous ways. And here is where AI makes things particularly tricky: if the attack gets blocked or the defences kick in, the AI adapts its approach and tries a different method. It is essentially a self-correcting attack. So what we are dealing with is not a one-shot attempt but a persistent, intelligent process that keeps evolving until it finds a way through.

Let's start with aviation, where the stakes are particularly high. Air traffic control systems can be overwhelmed with false aircraft position data, creating confusion about which planes are actually where in the sky. Flight management computers might receive incorrect weather information or routing data, potentially sending aircraft into dangerous conditions. During the most critical moments, takeoff and landing, attackers can target airport ground control systems when precision is absolutely essential. GPS spoofing is another serious threat, where aircraft receive false positioning signals that quietly steer them away from their intended flight paths. Perhaps most concerning, automated safety systems that pilots depend on can be bypassed through carefully coordinated signal manipulation, leaving crews flying blind when they need these systems most.

Maritime operations face their own unique vulnerabilities. Port management systems can be flooded with false cargo manifests and vessel schedules, creating chaos in some of the world's busiest shipping hubs. Ships themselves are vulnerable to navigation attacks through manipulated radar and GPS signals, which can send massive cargo vessels dangerously off course. Automated loading systems that handle millions of tonnes of cargo can be disrupted, creating bottlenecks that ripple through global supply chains. Maritime traffic control relies on accurate vessel position and speed data, but when that information is corrupted, the risk of collisions increases dramatically. And because shipping schedules are artificially altered, the entire supply chain coordination breaks down, affecting everything from fuel deliveries to food distribution.

Ground transport networks present a different set of challenges. Traffic signal timing can be manipulated to create deliberate congestion or even accidents at busy intersections. Railway systems are particularly vulnerable because signalling depends on accurate train position data, and false information here can lead to catastrophic collisions. Public transit faces attacks on scheduling algorithms, where fake passenger demand data causes buses and trains to be in completely the wrong places. Smart motorways that adjust speed limits and lane usage based on traffic flow can be fed incorrect information, creating dangerous driving conditions. And as more vehicles become connected to infrastructure through vehicle-to-infrastructure communication, these networks become new attack vectors that can affect thousands of vehicles simultaneously.

While each transport sector has specific vulnerabilities, there are patterns that appear across aviation, maritime, and ground transport. Many operators still rely on legacy systems with known security weaknesses that haven't been fully patched or updated. Real-time data feeds that all these systems depend on can be poisoned with false sensor information, causing them to respond to conditions that don't actually exist. Remote access interfaces are often poorly secured, which means attackers can exploit these systems from anywhere in the world without physical access. Because transport operations are so dependent on precise timing, coordinated delay tactics can cause cascading failures across entire networks. And increasingly, the integration points where different transport systems connect to each other are becoming prime targets, because a single successful attack can affect multiple sectors simultaneously.

So, now that we understand the threats, let's talk about what we can actually do to defend against them. The good news is that many of these measures are straightforward. First, keeping software and systems up to date is absolutely essential, because outdated systems are easy targets. You also want to be watching your network traffic constantly, so that anything unusual gets spotted quickly. Your people matter too, so make sure staff are properly trained to recognise suspicious activity and phishing attempts. Adding multi-factor authentication means that even if a password is stolen, attackers still can't easily get in. One of the most important steps is physically and digitally separating your critical control systems from general networks, so that if one part is compromised, it doesn't bring everything down. And finally, organisations should be sharing information about threats with each other and with government agencies, because collective knowledge makes everyone stronger. None of these steps are beyond reach, and together they build a genuinely robust defence.

So let's bring everything together from this lesson. The core message is this: AI-driven attacks on transport systems are not a future concern, they are happening right now, every single day. Whether it is navigation being fed false data or schedules being quietly manipulated, these threats are very real. And here is something worth remembering: even a small disruption can ripple outwards and cause serious consequences for thousands of people. So what can you do? Start by staying alert. If something about a system looks or feels unusual, do not ignore it. Trust that instinct. Report anything suspicious to your security team straight away, because early reporting can make an enormous difference. Beyond that, keep learning about these threats, share what you know with your colleagues, and stay prepared. Awareness really is your first and most powerful line of defence. In the next lesson, we will look at how organisations are beginning to fight back against these kinds of attacks.

Who is this for: Transport managers, logistics coordinators, fleet managers, operations supervisors, air traffic controllers, maritime officers, railway operators, shipping coordinators, automotive systems technicians, supply chain managers
Team or Dept this is for: Transportation, Operations, Logistics, Fleet Management, Supply Chain, Maritime Operations, Aviation, Railway Operations, Automotive, Infrastructure
